cards.title: mazer: AI run corpus quality, retention, and calibration
cards.markerName: AI run corpus quality and calibration
cards.summary: Turn account-scoped AI run receipts into versioned, quality-gated calibration cohorts without deleting raw evidence or automatically changing live progression.
cards.whyItMatters: The Fawxzzy account has more than 1,300 completed AI cycles, enough to reveal repeatable navigation, generator, and progression behavior. Mixed receipt schemas, incomplete remote coverage, wrapped-route benchmark drift, and duplicated scoring formulas can produce incorrect tuning unless the corpus is governed first.
cards.currentStatus: A 2026-07-11 read-only audit found 1,341 completed AI cycles and 1,139 durable receipts: 1,122 menu-demo and 17 play. There are no exact duplicates and storage is about 2.2 MB. Wave 1 landed on Mazer PR #37 with an export-only redacted quality audit. Wave 2 landed on Mazer PR #38 at merge commit `b380d2bc`: telemetry now benchmarks the legal wrap-aware playable graph while preserving generator `solutionPath`, and runtime, reporting, corpus audit, and calibration consume canonical scorer `mazer.maze-cycle-ai-decision-pressure` version `1.0.0`. Reports preserve historical stored scores separately from current recomputation. Full Mazer verification passed 323 tests across 42 files plus production build; fast verification passed 127 tests and the calibration smoke reached 5/5 goals with zero target leaks. Raw receipts and account progression remain immutable; future receipt versioning, idempotent coverage, seed packs, and offline formula calibration remain open.
workBreakdown: Create a versioned corpus audit that assigns analytical quality states and reason codes without modifying raw receipts.
Add receipt schema, build, AI algorithm, generator, benchmark-graph, run-origin, cycle-sequence, and progression-context metadata to future receipts.
Add an idempotent client receipt identity and bounded retry/outbox contract so remote receipt gaps are observable and retries cannot create duplicates.
Make runtime and Atlas reporting consume one canonical AI decision scorer.
Correct shortest-path and route-efficiency metrics to use the same traversable graph as actual player and AI movement, including wraps.
Generate representative, stress, and wrap-anomaly seed packs for deterministic before-and-after testing.
Replay candidate progression formulas offline against quality-approved cohorts without mutating account progression.
Define raw receipt retention, aggregate rollups, export, progression reset, and explicit history deletion as separate contracts.
nextActions: Write a durable read-only audit receipt with the current counts and quality findings.
Fix metric-contract correctness before changing progression thresholds.
Produce the first baseline report and seed packs from the preserved raw corpus.
acceptanceCriteria: Every receipt used for calibration has an explicit schema/build/algorithm cohort and quality classification.
Runtime and Atlas scoring produce identical AI pressure values and signal classifications for the same receipt.
The shortest-path benchmark uses the actual traversable graph and cannot exceed a valid completed path.
Remote receipt retries are idempotent and the difference between progression count and durable receipt coverage is explainable.
Raw receipts remain immutable; legacy and excluded rows are preserved with reason codes.
A deterministic seed pack reproduces representative and long-tail AI behavior.
Candidate progression changes are evaluated offline against the same fixed corpus before live adoption.
Menu AI, human play, QA, calibration, and replay runs are not silently mixed.
Retention and user-data deletion semantics are documented and tested.
No automatic online tuning or unreviewed weight promotion is introduced.
proofPlan: Run the corpus audit against a redacted Supabase export and verify counts against live read-only SQL.
Run scorer-parity tests across historical receipt fixtures.
Run wrapped-graph shortest-path invariants across the anomaly seed pack.
Run idempotent receipt retry and count-reconciliation tests.
Run counterfactual progression simulations without writing Supabase or local progression state.
Publish a bounded Atlas-safe report with no user id, secret, or unbounded path data.

cards.title: mazer: player, AI, rank, score, and maze progression contract
cards.markerName: Progression, score, rank, and maze-rating contract
cards.summary: Document and redesign how player and AI run scores, hidden ratings, rank, target complexity, maze rating, and level bands choose the next maze without punitive regression.
cards.whyItMatters: Progression must reward strong but imperfect runs, preserve meaningful rank differences, and keep player, AI, and generated-maze semantics explainable without exposing competing public metrics.
cards.currentStatus: The current contract stores separate player and AI-runner tracks, uses targetComplexity as the intended source of level/rank, and derives 0-100 paceScore from time, route efficiency, wrong turns, backtracks, reset use, render safety, and AI decision pressure. Current level bands are four target-complexity points wide and rank bands are E/D/C/B/A/S. The public UI still calls the player metric Skill Lvl in some surfaces. Mazer PR #38 removed wrapped-route benchmark and scorer-implementation disagreement for future/runtime recomputation without changing thresholds, rank bands, historical progression, or account state. Formula terminology, representative outcome fixtures, hysteresis, and quality-approved offline calibration remain open; rank-only presentation stays in its linked successor card.
workBreakdown: Inventory every stored, derived, displayed, and telemetry-only progression field before changing formulas or labels.
Define score, hidden rating, rank, level, band, target complexity, measured maze rating, difficulty, and complexity in one versioned contract.
Recalibrate time, route efficiency, wrong-turn, backtrack, reset, render-safety, and AI-decision weights against deterministic perfect, strong, average, weak, and failed runs.
Smooth temporary struggle so a single weak run cannot cause disproportionate regression while strong imperfect runs still make meaningful progress.
Keep player and AI tracks independent, name any intentionally different semantics, and retain save compatibility until an approved migration exists.
Keep rank S as the top rank while target complexity and visible level can continue toward level 99.
Replay current and candidate progression formulas only against purpose-approved historical cohorts, with robust time/outlier handling, topology-aware route pressure, and regression hysteresis.
nextActions: Publish the current formula and field inventory before changing thresholds.
Build deterministic progression fixtures and expected target-complexity deltas for five representative run outcomes.
Coordinate rank-only display work without deleting hidden player-skill/rating data.
Use validated player and AI receipts to calibrate bands only after maze-feature delivery is measured.
Compare current and candidate formula trajectories offline before proposing any live threshold, band, or weight change.
acceptanceCriteria: Score, skill, rating, rank, level, band, difficulty, complexity, and maze rating have explicit non-overlapping definitions.
Stored fields, derived fields, public labels, save compatibility, tests, and telemetry consumers are documented before migration or formula changes.
Perfect, strong, average, weak, and failed runs produce deterministic expected score, signal, and target-complexity outcomes.
Strong but imperfect completion can progress while one temporary weak run does not create disproportionate regression.
Rank and level-band boundaries are centralized, explainable, and covered by deterministic tests.
Measured maze rating is compared to requested target complexity rather than treated as an arbitrary label.
Player and AI formulas explicitly identify shared terms versus intentionally distinct behavior terms.
No public surface presents two competing progression values to the player.
Candidate formulas are evaluated against fixed quality-approved cohorts without mutating the Fawxzzy account or live progression state.
proofPlan: Run focused progression, telemetry, and marker tests with fixture receipts across every signal outcome.
Inspect player and AI cycle receipts for score terms, route benchmark values, generation delivery, rank, and level derivation.
Capture mobile and desktop badge surfaces after the linked rank-only display card is implemented.

cards.title: mazer: human-configured AI runner
cards.summary: Replace solver-like behavior with a human-style runner that perceives, classifies, commits, doubts, recovers, and learns without secretly routing through the full maze graph.
cards.whyItMatters: The AI is the game's tuning dummy. If it knows the end or receives a hidden full-floor route, score, rank, maze difficulty, and player-facing complexity all become misleading.
cards.currentStatus: The hidden full-floor emergency fallback is removed; A*/shortest-path data is a post-run benchmark; and the AI exposes thought, choice, confidence, remembered options, and a non-goal target. Human-memory recovery can cross a previously exhausted but remembered corridor to reach a verified unexplored frontier, while dead ends remain excluded from new branch selection. Generated menu AI no longer emits ai-path-exhausted or regenerates before goal; focused 30-maze proof and a 60-case E-rank calibration both reached goal in every case with zero target leaks and zero pre-goal regenerations. The rank controller now has a real D-through-S local cognition ladder instead of sharing D behavior: local lookahead grows, confidence noise/split uncertainty decrease, and wrap mental cost drops without granting a solver route. The maintained E-to-S sweep is monotonic, moving average score 65.2 to 80.8 and route-efficiency pressure 61.98 to 25.937. The latest recovery tuning pass rejected E local-progress scoring, a bounded endpoint-discovery prototype, recovery penalties 0.8 and 0.3, and a bounded corridor-terminal preview because each worsened the route-efficiency baseline; all changes were reverted. Decision-level recovery diagnostics now record source/split/target, known-route length, confidence, choice class, and thought state for every recovery. The restored 60-case E baseline reports 1,239 frontier recoveries, 120 optional retargets, 11.586 average known-route steps, and a 105-step maximum. Built-in scale summaries isolate the long tail in the scale-75 stress band: route ratio 6.05, 13.668 recovery steps, and p90 ratio 14.059. The tutorial profile currently targets scale 29, so this is a future progression handoff threshold rather than an early-game assignment. A scale-75 E-through-S sweep reaches all 30 goals with zero leaks but fails rank monotonicity: B/A pressure is 61.371, while C/S regress to 80.947. Calibration now also requires monotonic raw route ratio: E/D/C/B/A/S is 4.179/4.978/5.616/4.335/4.131/4.38, so score or pressure alone cannot certify a slower controller. The first preview action worsened B 4.335 to 5.197 and S 4.38 to 4.645, while A only improved 4.131 to 4.116, so solvePreviewBudget remains diagnostics-only. Long E-rank searches remain intentionally fallible and score poorly through route-efficiency pressure; the next controller change must be ledger-driven before high-scale promotion. The imported corpus can now provide behavior-ready clean/searching/chaotic cohorts and deterministic long-tail seeds, but not automatic online learning or live controller promotion.
workBreakdown: Use a noisy vision model: clear corridors are trusted more than dense intersections, wraps, and near-overlapping paths.
Separate perception from route choice by labeling branches as promising, risky, dead-end-looking, shortcut-looking, or unclear before selecting a move.
Keep A*/shortest path only as an internal benchmark for scoring after a run.
Add rank-based lookahead budgets from low-rank local junction checks to high-rank partial top-down planning.
Use implemented human bias profiles such as direct-chaser, wall-follower, shortcut-gambler, cautious mapper, and speedrunner.
Use confidence to decide whether to commit, doubt, retarget to a remembered split, or recover from a bad branch.
Apply mental cost to wrap paths so folded-world shortcuts become easier only as skill/rank improves.
Expose clean visible thought states: exploring, committing, doubting, recovering, shortcut-testing, and goal-confirming.
Replace any route-to-end fallback with known-frontier expansion.
Color active non-goal target tiles by current thought state while keeping remembered options visible as distinct memory markers.
Use branch-decision diagnostics to compare local candidate score, confidence, class, and sanitized post-run canonical alignment without exposing an end target or solver route.
Use recovery-target diagnostics to compare frontier-pool size and selected-score margin without logging alternate target coordinates.

Maintain npm run ai:calibrate, npm run ai:calibrate:ranks, and npm run ai:calibrate:biases as repeatable generated-seed proof for raw route ratio, reach/regeneration counts, invalid moves, goal-target leaks, thought states, choice classes, resolved perception profile, rank-sweep average-score comparison, route-efficiency-pressure comparison, and bias-profile separation.
Add richer UI visualization beyond the amber target tile, including branch labels, remembered options, target confidence, and current thought state.
Generate representative, stress, single-route, and wrap-anomaly seed packs from quality-approved receipts so controller changes compare against fixed controls instead of only fresh random samples.
nextActions: Test one D-rank committed-frontier or candidate-pool-reduction policy against raw route ratio, then tune C's lower canonical branch alignment separately; do not apply another shared rank weight or repeat the rejected preview/global-vision/path-penalty experiments.
Retain the no-cutoff invariant: pre-goal local-memory exhaustion must not reset, regenerate, teleport, or target the end.
Expose the now-distinct rank cognition in a compact player-facing thought/skill legend without overwhelming normal UI.
Keep default E-rank calibration near the healthier challenge/ease/hold mix while reducing regeneration count through route-quality improvements.
Run longer rank samples after each controller/generator tuning pass and compare goalTargetLeakPass, monotonicAverageScorePass, monotonicAverageRouteRatioPass, and monotonicRouteEfficiencyPass.
Add a compact player-facing legend for the thought-state colors and target-confidence visualization.
Review clean/searching/chaotic and long-tail behavior cohorts before any AI weight promotion; do not add automatic online learning.
acceptanceCriteria: The AI cannot use a hidden full-floor route or reset/regenerate shortcut to reach the end after local memory fails.
The AI never exposes the end as its target before it has actually confirmed or reached it.
AI scoring compares against shortest viable path after the run without using that route to control movement.
The maintained calibration command reports zero goal-target leaks and zero invalid moves across the default generated-seed sample.
Visible thought overlays explain why the AI is exploring, committing, doubting, recovering, or testing a shortcut.
Runtime diagnostics expose thought state, choice class, confidence, option points, and non-goal target point for screenshot QA.
Low-rank AI remains fallible while high-rank AI can plan larger chunks without becoming a perfect solver.
proofPlan: Run focused known-frontier tests for no hidden goal target, no pre-goal cutoff, and goal-only regeneration.
Run npm run ai:calibrate -- --summary-only and inspect route ratio, leak, reach, regeneration, score, signal, level, rank, thought-state, and choice-class summaries.
Run generated multi-seed AI samples and inspect score/progression movement.
Capture menu AI screenshots showing remembered options, non-goal targets, and thought-state visualization.

cards.title: mazer: edge-wrap topology, border fill, and notch safety
cards.summary: Make wrapped border paths a real graph contract, keep start/goal/navigation aware of wrap edges, and prevent maze path visuals from entering border cutouts.
cards.whyItMatters: Wrap paths and border notches change both visuals and navigation. They must be graph-owned, not just visual tricks, or AI distance, player movement, and difficulty scoring drift apart.
cards.currentStatus: Mazer PR #38 landed explicit direct-floor and playable-wrap-aware shortest-path policies, deterministic unreachable semantics, self-wrap rejection, and telemetry migration to the legal playable benchmark while preserving generator `solutionPath`. Focused graph and telemetry fixtures plus the full 323-test verification spine pass. Generation guarantees, border-notch rendering/fill, bleed-path feature classification, and retrospective re-export of historical route benchmarks remain open.
workBreakdown: Guarantee at least one horizontal and one vertical wrap candidate only when legal for the current maze bounds.
Represent wrap neighbors in the graph so AI, player movement, compass, trail, and complexity formulas all agree.
Keep generated paths out of title/compass notches and folded-corner voids.
Improve bottom and corner fill so paths reach usable border edges without clipping into decorative cutouts.
Classify bleed-off edge paths as always-on, level-gated, mode-specific, experimental, deprecated, or disconnected instead of inferring intent from absence.
Compute shortest path from the same legal neighbor contract used by movement, telemetry, AI, and player controls, including wraps.
nextActions: Add topology diagnostics for wrap-pair validity.
Add notch-safe render masks or generator constraints.
Capture visual proof for top notch, bottom notch, and all four folded corners.
Feed confirmed edge-path classification into the linked feature progression matrix.
Build a fixed wrap-shortcut anomaly pack and prove a completed path cannot undercut the computed graph shortest path.
acceptanceCriteria: At least one horizontal and one vertical wrap candidate can be generated when complexity allows it.
AI distance and route choice account for wrap neighbors instead of treating them as visual-only paths.
Path rendering respects the folded border notches and fills toward edges without leaking into cutout voids.
Bleed-off path availability, level-band introduction, mode behavior, and complexity contribution are explicitly documented.
A graph shortest-path invariant holds for representative and historical wrap-anomaly fixtures.

cards.title: mazer: fluid controls, player motion, and path feel
cards.summary: Replace snappy tile jumps and hard-coded stick segments with smoother player motion, dynamic directional intent, and bounded assistance through unambiguous zigzag corridors.
cards.whyItMatters: Fast timed play will fail if the controls feel segmented, sticky, or jumpy. The player should feel like they are steering through a path, not stepping through a debug grid.
cards.currentStatus: Tap/hold parity, canonical movement plumbing, ordered stick candidates, and wrap snap improvements exist. The current controller can select legal adjacent moves but does not yet preserve a macro direction through a bounded, unambiguous alternating corridor; that behavior must not become branch selection or wall clipping.
workBreakdown: Make stick angle represent movement intent rather than hard-coded segment commands.
Resolve intent against legal graph neighbors dynamically every movement step.
Smooth visual player motion while preserving exact maze-cell legality.
Keep single-click, hold, keyboard, on-screen arrows, and stick behavior consistent.
Define macro direction, bounded lookahead, and explicit-input precedence for short horizontal and vertical zigzag corridors.
nextActions: Design the dynamic intent resolver before changing input visuals again.
Add QA scripts for tap, hold, diagonal-ish intent, blocked wall, release, and wrap snap cases.
Tune movement speed with pace/level formulas instead of one fixed feel.
Add deterministic fixtures for simple zigzags, ambiguous junctions, dead ends, reverse turns, narrow corridors, and repeated alternating turns.
acceptanceCriteria: Single presses move exactly once while holds continue only while active.

proofPlan: Run live play QA for quick taps, holds, stick drags, and wrap paths.

cards.title: mazer: animation smoothness and lifecycle cadence
cards.summary: Remove visible pauses, loop resets, and buffered animation stalls across title orbit, scanner, background, border glimmer, trail pulse, build, and deconstruct.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer depends on continuous motion. Any hitch during build/deconstruct or title/background loops makes the whole app feel laggy even when gameplay logic is correct.
cards.currentStatus: Several frame cadence fixes landed, but mobile soak and lifecycle-boundary jitter review remain open.

Patch any remaining timer or tween coupling that pauses global animations.
acceptanceCriteria: No visible title, compass, or background freeze occurs during maze handoff.
Build and deconstruct keep crisp staged motion on mobile and desktop.

proofPlan: Record route-aware screenshots or video frames through a full menu and play lifecycle.
Compare frame timing before and after animation handoff changes.

cards.title: mazer: play-mode perpetual completion loop
cards.summary: Keep players locked into play after reaching the end by recording the cycle, deconstructing cleanly, rebuilding a fresh maze, then restarting play without returning to menu.
cards.whyItMatters: The core game loop should be endless and progression-backed. Completion should feel like a polished arcade handoff, not a reset or dead stop.
cards.currentStatus: First lifecycle pass works in production proof: QA can drive to goal, observe goal-hold, deconstruct, handoff, fresh build, compass spin, and ready state. Title parity, timing polish, and longer held-control soak remain.
workBreakdown: Record completion and shortest-path comparison before the next maze starts.
Lock input during deconstruct/build while keeping compass and ambient motion active.
Generate each next player maze from current skill, score, and target complexity.
Keep Reset/Menu in pause as explicit player choices.
nextActions: Polish deconstruct timing after player goal contact.
Decide whether play mode gets the Mazer title above the maze.
Run longer held-stick tests across lifecycle boundaries.
acceptanceCriteria: After reaching the end, play mode deconstructs and rebuilds a fresh procedural maze.
The timer excludes build time and restarts only when the new maze is playable.
Player progress is saved before returning to menu or closing the app.
proofPlan: Drive a real play maze to the goal with runtime QA.
Verify post-goal lifecycle pass, fresh seed, locked input during build, and timer reset.

cards.title: mazer: iridescent player, trail, and pulse material

cards.title: mazer: diagonal path graph contract

cards.title: mazer: route-aware visual proof discipline

cards.title: mazer: player input and movement correctness

cards.whyItMatters: Skill, score, route efficiency, and maze difficulty all depend on movement being fair and measurable. Input bugs poison progression analytics.
cards.currentStatus: Shared input plumbing, repeat gating, pause locks, and diagonal legality tests exist. QA bridge moves, pointer swipe releases, and buffered keyboard held diagonals now route through the same one-visible-step diagonal planner as touch controls; full browser/mobile proof remains open.
workBreakdown: Audit keydown, keyup, browser repeat, and duplicate listener paths. First pass complete for buffered keyboard path.

Preserve pause/input-lock behavior.

Soak keyboard repeat and stick repeat rates against diagnostics.
Keep true diagonal pathing deferred until generation, graph, trail, compass, and AI distance share one contract.

cards.title: mazer: maze ratings, difficulty bands, and generator shaping
cards.markerName: Maze rating, difficulty bands, and generator shaping
cards.summary: Turn player and AI progression targets into measurable maze ratings, difficulty bands, and generated structure instead of badge-only labels.
cards.whyItMatters: The endless loop needs mazes that actually match requested complexity, with a measured rating that explains why a band is easy or hard and when a candidate should be rejected or retried.
cards.currentStatus: Difficulty profiles currently map target complexity into scale, checkpoint pressure, shortcut pressure, route-quality reinforcement, required opposite-border wraps, and perimeter feeder pressure. Mazer PR #38 now separates generator-authored `solutionPath` from the legal playable shortest-path benchmark so measured route pressure cannot silently use direct-only movement when wraps are legal. Requested/measured/experienced terminology, boundary calibration, feature-matrix ownership, and quality-approved long-run reconciliation remain open; scale 75 stays an AI stress band rather than a promotion target.
workBreakdown: Define tutorial, starter, explorer, navigator, architect, and mythic difficulty profiles.
Map the active player or AI track into executable generation pressure terms.
Expose generationReview with under-target, on-target, and over-target classification.
Select from a bounded seed window instead of blindly accepting the first generated maze.

Persist bounded requested target, rank/level before run, generation profile/band, delivery review, and benchmark-graph version so retrospective cohorts can distinguish requested from experienced difficulty.
nextActions: Define and prove the future skill-to-scale handoff that admits scale-75 topology only after the matching AI rank has monotonic raw route-ratio and recovery-pressure proof at that scale.
Tune branch, fill, dead-end, and wrap-path pressure against longer samples.

acceptanceCriteria: Generated maze complexity tracks the active player or AI target band within a bounded search cost.

Long-run proof shows difficulty changes remain readable and stable instead of jumping or stalling.

proofPlan: Run progression and generation tests for difficulty profile selection.

cards.whyItMatters: A stealth item creates a readable emergency escape tool without changing the core maze graph or making enemies permanently harmless.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. Duration, spawn source, visual language, cooldown, stacking, and multiplayer fairness rules still need design before implementation.
workBreakdown: Represent cloak duration as turn/time bounded state on the player.
Mask the cloaked player from enemy compass, tracker, pursuit targeting, and retarget scoring while active.
Freeze all enemy movement in the maze until the cloak duration completes.
Allow the cloaked player to pass through enemies without collision failure during the active window.
Show clear activation, remaining duration, and expiry feedback without exposing debug terms.
Define whether AI-controlled players can use the cloak and how enemy AI reacts after expiry.
nextActions: Choose duration units and whether duration decrements by real time, accepted turns, or game ticks.
Define pickup source: mystery box, item inventory, direct spawn, or game-mode-specific grant.
Design player/enemy visual states for cloaked, frozen, pass-through, and expiry frames.
Write collision and enemy-targeting rules before adding art.
acceptanceCriteria: Enemy tracker and compass cannot target a cloaked player during the active duration.
Enemy movement stays frozen for the whole cloak duration and resumes deterministically after expiry.
Player/enemy collision is pass-through while cloaked and returns to normal after expiry.
The cloak effect is bounded, visible to the player, and covered by deterministic tests.
proofPlan: Run turn/duration unit tests for activation, countdown, expiry, and enemy resume.
Run enemy targeting tests proving cloaked players are excluded from pursuit decisions.
Run collision tests proving pass-through only applies during the active cloak window.
Run visual proof for activation, countdown, frozen enemies, and expiry.

cards.title: mazer: multiplayer foundation
cards.summary: Add the session, identity, color, visibility, and sync contracts needed for two-player Mazer without assuming a final game mode.
cards.whyItMatters: Multiplayer changes progression, rendering, trails, collisions, and network truth; it needs a foundation before survival or moving-maze modes depend on it.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. The intended local presentation is self-is-green and other-player-is-red on each device, with the other player trail hidden or always short-fading.
workBreakdown: Define multiplayer session creation, joining, identity, reconnect, and leave behavior.
Render the current user as green on their own device and the other participant as red.
Render the other participant as green on their device and this user as red there.
Hide the other player trail by default or show only a short fading trail.
Define latency, input authority, collision, item ownership, enemy targeting, and progression receipt boundaries.
Keep player-one/player-two labels local to each device instead of treating one device as globally primary.
nextActions: Choose network transport and state authority model.
Define how multiplayer receipts differ from solo and AI-runner receipts.
Design color/trail rules for accessibility and colorblind alternatives.
Decide whether multiplayer is account-only, guest-compatible, or mixed.
acceptanceCriteria: Each device sees itself as green and the other player as red.
Other-player trail is hidden or short-fading and cannot clutter route readability.
Session state survives reasonable reconnects or reports a clean leave/fail state.
Items, enemy targeting, collisions, and receipts have explicit multiplayer ownership rules.
proofPlan: Run two-client session state tests with mirrored color roles.
Run trail-visibility tests for self and other player views.
Run deterministic conflict tests for simultaneous movement, item pickup, and enemy targeting.

cards.title: mazer: endless multiplayer survival mode
cards.summary: Add an endless survival game mode where enemies and items spawn over time and players survive as long as possible, with solo and multiplayer settings.
cards.whyItMatters: Mazer needs a long-form mode that uses enemies, items, and multiplayer as a survival loop instead of only endpoint completion.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. The mode should support solo first if needed, then multiplayer where each device treats its own user as green and the other participant as red.
workBreakdown: Create a no-end survival objective with duration, score, wave, or survival-time tracking.
Spawn enemies over time with bounded density and difficulty scaling.
Spawn usable items over time, including future cloak and mystery-box items.
Support multiplayer role mirroring with self green and other player red on each device.
Keep other-player trail hidden or short-fading so survival readability stays clean.
Define whether players cooperate, compete, revive, collide, or share enemy pressure.
nextActions: Define survival scoring and fail conditions.
Choose spawn pacing for enemies and items by elapsed time, maze pressure, and player count.
Design HUD for survival time, wave pressure, item slot, and teammate/opponent state.
Prototype solo survival before enabling multiplayer if network foundation is not ready.
acceptanceCriteria: The mode has no endpoint clear condition and measures survival duration or score.
Enemy and item spawn rates stay bounded and readable over long sessions.
Multiplayer rendering follows local self-green/other-red rules on both devices.
The mode has deterministic tests for spawn cadence, collision/failure, item pickup, and multiplayer visibility.
proofPlan: Run survival-mode state-machine tests for spawn, scoring, and fail transitions.
Run long-session simulation tests for enemy and item density caps.
Run two-client visual proof for mirrored player colors and trail behavior.

cards.title: mazer: moving procedural maze mode
cards.summary: Add a mode where the maze pans in one or more directions while new maze space procedurally generates ahead of the player.
cards.whyItMatters: A moving maze can create pressure without a fixed endpoint, but it requires streaming topology, camera, AI, trail, and spawn rules to agree.
cards.currentStatus: Backlog only. Direction policy, generation window, wrap behavior, enemy/item spawn ownership, and fail conditions are unresolved.
workBreakdown: Define whether the maze pans in one fixed direction, changes direction, or supports multiple simultaneous directional pressures.
Stream procedural maze cells ahead of the pan while retiring or locking cells behind the player.
Keep player movement, enemy movement, items, compass, trails, and camera aligned with the moving world frame.
Define failure when the player is pushed out, trapped, caught, or falls behind the generated safe region.
Make panning speed scale separately from maze difficulty and survival pressure.
nextActions: Design the world-window data structure before rendering the panning effect.
Decide if this is solo, multiplayer, or a variant of endless survival.
Prototype deterministic generated chunks with stable joins and no disconnected safe path.
Define enemy and item spawn rules for newly generated maze bands.
acceptanceCriteria: New maze space generates before it is needed and remains connected to the playable region.
Panning movement does not desync player, enemies, trails, items, or camera bounds.
Failure and scoring rules are explicit and deterministic.
The mode can be tested with fixed seeds and panning schedules.
proofPlan: Run chunk-generation tests for continuity across pan boundaries.
Run camera/world-frame tests proving player/enemy/item positions stay aligned.
Run visual proof for one-direction and direction-change panning schedules.

cards.title: mazer: rank-only player progression display
cards.summary: Remove player-facing Skill and Player Skill labels and present one consistent public rank display without deleting hidden progression data.
cards.whyItMatters: Players should not have to reconcile skill, rating, rank, level, and score as competing public measures. Rank is the chosen public progression signal; score remains run quality.
cards.currentStatus: The current HUD, Options guide, and some player-facing copy still use Skill Lvl terminology. The existing progression record stores more than the public UI needs, so this is a terminology and surface-audit delivery, not a data-deletion task.
workBreakdown: Audit HUD, main menu, Options, Pause, completion/results, onboarding, tooltips, accessibility labels, and mobile layouts for player Skill or Player Skill wording.
Replace the public player progression display with rank-only language while preserving score as a separate 0-100 run-quality value.
Keep any internal skill/rating fields hidden and backward-compatible until the progression contract approves a migration.
Make labels, compact badge layouts, and guide explanations consistent across desktop and phone surfaces.
nextActions: Use the progression terminology audit as the source for exact public labels and descriptions.
Build a surface inventory before changing any storage schema or telemetry field.
Capture authenticated menu, Options, play, and Pause proofs at desktop and phone sizes.
acceptanceCriteria: No normal player-facing surface exposes Player Skill, Skill Lvl, or a redundant public rating label.
Rank is presented consistently wherever player progression is shown, and score is clearly described as run quality rather than rank.
Hidden fields, saves, telemetry, and account-scoped progression remain compatible until a deliberate migration is approved.
Updated compact badges, guide copy, and accessibility labels fit at supported desktop and mobile viewports.
Regression tests cover public terminology and the absence of legacy player-skill labels.
proofPlan: Run focused UI terminology and progression tests.
Run authenticated `npm run visual:ui-surfaces` captures for menu, Options, play, and Pause at phone and desktop sizes.
Read runtime diagnostics to confirm hidden progression fields still serialize without becoming public copy.

cards.title: mazer: cross-viewport UI reliability and layout hardening
cards.summary: Coordinate the mobile safe-area/portrait and browser layout-persistence lanes through one viewport contract and proof matrix without turning either delivery into an offset patch.
cards.whyItMatters: The same UI can be valid in a desktop canvas, narrow browser, mobile browser, installed PWA, or restored window only if viewport state, safe areas, and restoration behavior share deliberate rules.
cards.currentStatus: Merged Mazer PR #39 implements explicit title -> maze -> rank -> actions menu lanes and top rank/Pause HUD -> maze -> controls play lanes, caps maze growth by remaining usable content, and anchors the canvas to normalized visual-viewport and safe-area origins. The pure solver restored identical phone bounds after a 390x844 -> 1440x900 -> 390x844 transition; phone menu/play and the four-platform matrix reported zero overlap/offscreen violations. Remaining authenticated overlay endpoints, real-device inset proof, and installed-PWA behavior keep this at 78.
workBreakdown: Define canonical viewport, safe-area, visible-height, and no-overlap invariants for menu, Options, play, Pause, dialogs, HUD, controls, and scroll rails.
Maintain the child boundary between mobile shell/device behavior and browser layout-state persistence.
Create a verified viewport matrix covering narrow/wide desktop, resize/restore, phone browser, installed PWA where available, and authenticated overlays.
Require route, mode, viewport, safe-area state, scroll position, and layout diagnostics beside visual proof.
nextActions: Extend the normalized-geometry transition matrix across narrow/wide, maximize/restore, phone-like, and overlay states.
Capture guest and authenticated Options/Play/Pause at 360x720 and 405x958 after transition endpoints.
Use the canonical geometry owner rather than raw per-overlay viewport reads or UI offsets.
Keep mazer-visual-proof-discipline as the proof contract rather than treating screenshots as a substitute for state checks.
acceptanceCriteria: Every primary Mazer surface has an owner, supported viewport category, and no-overlap contract.
Mobile and browser layout work can be verified independently and then together through a shared matrix.
No completion claim relies on desktop-only proof for a phone or restored-window defect.
The board records direct links to the mobile, browser-layout, guide, and proof cards.
proofPlan: Run the agreed layout matrix with authenticated and guest surface coverage.
Review generated proof metadata for route, viewport, mode, overlay, safe-area, and scroll state.
Read back linked card status before ratcheting this coordinating marker.

cards.title: mazer: normalize browser UI layout across viewports
cards.summary: Normalize responsive browser UI geometry so a narrow-window layout cannot overlap, escape, or expand incorrectly after resize, maximize, restore, or reload. Persist semantic intent only if the product later introduces it.
cards.whyItMatters: The root-cause pass proved that Mazer has no user-adjustable panel geometry to restore. Derived dimensions must be recomputed from one viewport snapshot instead of being saved and replayed across browser states.
cards.currentStatus: Merged Mazer PR #39 keeps derived geometry non-persistent, replaces ratio-first sizing with a pure lane solver, and proves deterministic narrow-to-wide-to-narrow restoration. Side-tab 390x844 -> 1440x900 -> 390x844 readback restored title, board, and rank bounds exactly; macOS and Windows browser matrix targets passed with zero overlap/offscreen violations. Native maximize/restore automation, browser-chrome changes, and all authenticated overlays remain open, so this card advances to 75.
workBreakdown: Document the intentional no-persisted-geometry contract and keep semantic toggle/account preference state separate.
Normalize layout-derived state on reload, resize, maximize, restore, narrow-to-wide, wide-to-narrow, mobile emulation, and orientation transitions.
Reject stale geometry by recomputing from the active content rectangle instead of restoring overlap or off-screen controls.
Introduce a versioned semantic layout schema only if a supported user-adjustable intent is added later.
nextActions: Run deterministic desktop reload, narrow-to-wide, wide-to-narrow, maximize, and restore transition fixtures.
Classify any future state as semantic user intent, transient overlay state, account preference, or viewport-derived geometry before persisting it.
Add a migration/fallback matrix only if supported semantic layout state is introduced.
Validate browser behavior separately from mobile safe-area work, then feed both into the cross-viewport proof matrix.
acceptanceCriteria: The intentional no-persisted-geometry contract is explicit; any future semantic state owner and schema are versioned or safely migrated.
A layout recalculated after a different viewport cannot overlap, escape the viewport, cover essential controls, or fight responsive defaults.
Normal reload preserves supported semantic state without persisting invalid geometry.
Narrow/wide transitions, restore/maximize, mobile emulation, and orientation transitions have deterministic expected outcomes.
Invalid or obsolete state fails safely to a known default and is covered by regression tests.
proofPlan: Run viewport-transition tests for reload, narrow-to-wide, wide-to-narrow, maximize, restore, and corrupted legacy state.
Capture menu, Options, play, Pause, and confirmation-dialog proof at the relevant viewport endpoints.
Verify no-overlap diagnostics and actual browser screenshots before claiming persistence works.

cards.title: mazer: maze feature progression and mode parity
cards.summary: Audit and normalize maze-feature introduction across level bands and menu/play modes through one traceable feature registry or equivalent single source of truth.
cards.whyItMatters: A numeric difficulty profile is not enough when preview and playable mazes introduce different features or edge paths silently disappear. Every feature must have an intentional band, mode, and difficulty contribution.
cards.currentStatus: Current profiles already control scale, checkpoints, shortcuts, wrap requirements, route reinforcement, and perimeter feeders. Menu and play have separate build kinds while sharing high-level selection. There is no complete feature inventory/matrix proving what is always-on, level-gated, mode-specific, experimental, deprecated, or accidentally disconnected. The corpus lane will require future run receipts to retain a bounded feature-set or feature-registry version so topology outcomes can be analyzed by level band without guessing from timestamps.
workBreakdown: Inventory every actual maze-generation, graph, rendering, and parity feature with its code owner and current menu/play behavior.
Create a feature matrix with availability, level-band introduction, parameter range, difficulty contribution, mode compatibility, and intentional exclusion reason.
Establish one canonical feature registry or equivalent shared configuration consumed wherever menu/play parity is intended.
Classify bleed-off edge paths explicitly and decide whether they are lower-level only, later-gated, band-scaled, preview-only, playable, retired, or otherwise excluded.
Identify dead/unreachable configuration and duplicated mode-specific rules before copying settings between pipelines.
Link future run receipts to the enabled feature set or feature-registry version for mode, band, and topology cohort analysis.
nextActions: Audit menu and play entry points plus feature consumers before creating smaller implementation children.
Use the existing generator and edge-topology cards as linked implementation owners for confirmed findings.
Add a child card only when registry, parity, or bleed behavior proves to have a separate delivery boundary.
Supply feature-registry/version evidence to the corpus lane so generated topology outcomes are not silently mixed across feature eras.
acceptanceCriteria: All relevant maze-generation and rendering entry points are identified and mapped to actual features.
Every supported feature has an explicit progression and mode classification.
Bleed-off edge-path behavior, level-band introduction, and complexity contribution are a documented decision rather than inferred from absence.
Preview and playable paths share configuration where parity is intended, while intentional differences are explicit.
Boundary tests cover adjacent feature-introduction levels/bands and supported mode combinations.
Dead or duplicated configuration is removed or justified before the card is completed.
Historical and future topology cohorts can identify their feature registry or explicit legacy classification.
proofPlan: Generate a feature-matrix report for low, boundary, mid, and high difficulty bands in menu and play modes.
Run deterministic generation and topology tests for every supported feature class.
Capture representative menu/play visual pairs and record intentional differences beside the matrix.

cards.title: mazer: green player/trail and white shine readability lock
cards.markerName: Fixed green player/trail and white shine
cards.summary: Ship one fixed readability contract: a green player and trail with one white fading trail shine at the existing pulse cadence, separated from future iridescent material experiments.
cards.whyItMatters: The active player and trail must be immediately readable on every maze material tier. A fixed release contract prevents visual experimentation from changing gameplay recognition or adding cosmetic scope.
cards.currentStatus: The current baseline uses a green player/trail and one purple pulse. The latest visual requirement selects one white fading shine instead, retaining the existing 2600ms travel period and 33ms redraw cadence unless visual proof demonstrates a readability reason to change it. Rainbow, shop, cosmetic unlock, and material-tier work remain out of scope.
workBreakdown: Lock player core and trail to the high-contrast green readability anchor on menu and play surfaces.
Replace the purple pulse with exactly one white fading shine that follows the existing travel cadence and never competes with a second trail animation.
Preserve distinct colors for AI target, AI memory, start, end, maze paths, and trail overlays.
Add dense-maze contrast rules and diagnostics so path material changes cannot make the player or trail ambiguous.
nextActions: Audit every player/trail render path before changing colors or timing.
Capture dense menu and play mazes with player, trail, white shine, memory, and target overlays simultaneously visible.
Keep future iridescent work behind its separate material card after this fixed baseline is proven.
acceptanceCriteria: Player core and trail remain green and visibly distinct from maze path materials at supported contrast tiers.
Exactly one white fading trail shine is active; no purple pulse or competing moving shine remains in the fixed contract.
The shine preserves the current pulse cadence unless a documented readability test justifies a change.
AI target, memory, start, end, and player/trail colors remain semantically distinct in dense mazes.
Material tests and route-aware visual proof cover menu and play at phone and desktop widths.
proofPlan: Run material unit tests and dense-maze visual smoke for menu and play.
Capture phone and desktop proof with diagnostics that identify player, trail, shine, target, and memory colors.
Compare the fixed baseline against a pale-path maze before closing the card.

cards.title: mazer: auth UI and flow hardening
cards.summary: Harden login and account-creation presentation, native input geometry, mobile keyboard behavior, autofill, password-manager integration, and player-safe error states without changing auth ownership.
cards.whyItMatters: Authentication is the front door to account-backed play. Input overlays that shift behind browser chrome, fight the software keyboard, or expose raw provider errors can block otherwise healthy persistence and progression systems.
cards.currentStatus: The existing auth gate and persistent-login card owns session and state continuity at 94%, while account-scoped settings owns preference identity. This successor owns UI and flow hardening only. Implementation is intentionally deferred until the cross-viewport layout contract is stable because MenuScene, native auth inputs, overlay geometry, safe areas, and mobile viewport behavior overlap. Fitness may be reviewed as a proven reference, but any pattern must be deliberately adapted to Mazer rather than copied blindly.
workBreakdown: Audit login, account creation, session restoration, logout, and error-state presentation without changing the auth provider contract.
Place native email/password inputs from canonical viewport and safe-area geometry instead of independent DOM offsets.
Keep focused fields, submit actions, validation, and recovery controls reachable when iOS or Android software keyboards resize the visual viewport.
Define deliberate autocomplete, autofill, password-manager, enter-key, and focus-order behavior.
Translate provider and network failures into bounded player-safe messages while retaining diagnosable internal reason codes.
Review Fitness auth patterns as evidence and adapt only the parts that fit Mazer's canvas-plus-native-input architecture.
Capture authenticated and unauthenticated phone/desktop proof across login, creation, validation, recovery, and restored-session states.
nextActions: Wait for the cross-platform UI lane to stabilize menu and overlay geometry.
Inventory the existing native input bridge and auth-state transitions before changing presentation.
Build a focused keyboard/autofill/error-state matrix using Mazer routes and QA-safe accounts.
acceptanceCriteria: Login and account creation use one coherent player-facing flow with no duplicate state owners.
Native fields align with the visible auth surface after resize, browser chrome changes, safe-area changes, and software-keyboard open/close transitions.
Autofill and password managers identify fields correctly without obscuring controls or bypassing required validation.
Keyboard, touch, and desktop focus/submit behavior remain accessible and deterministic.
Provider details and secrets are never shown to players; errors remain useful, bounded, and testable.
Authenticated session restoration remains compatible with existing account-backed progression and settings.
proofPlan: Run focused auth, account-settings, and viewport tests without live user-data mutation.
Capture desktop and phone login/create-account flows with software keyboard, autofill, validation, and restored-session states.
Verify route, overlay, viewport, safe-area, focus, and auth-state diagnostics before moving the marker.
